title: Layout & Planning
introduction: Good trim work starts with thorough planning. Surveying conditions, planning sequences, and calculating materials before you cut saves time and prevents callbacks.
learningObjectives: Survey rooms and identify layout challenges before cutting
Plan trim sequences to minimize visible joints
Calculate material quantities with appropriate waste factors
Identify out-of-square and out-of-level conditions and plan corrections

criticalStandards.description: Always check walls for plumb and floors for level before cutting — never assume square
Plan scarf joints to fall over studs and face away from primary sight lines

procedures.title: Room Survey Protocol
procedures.body: Before cutting any trim, survey each room to identify conditions that affect layout and joinery.
steps: Check floor for level using 4' level — note high and low spots
Check walls for plumb at corners and door locations
Check ceiling for level if installing crown
Measure room dimensions and note irregularities
Identify stud locations (use stud finder, confirm with probe)
Note any obstructions: outlets, vents, pipes, built-ins
Photograph any pre-existing damage or conditions

callouts.title: The 10-Minute Investment
callouts.content: Spending 10 minutes surveying a room prevents hours of rework. Most trim problems come from cutting without checking conditions first.

procedures.title: Trim Sequence Planning
procedures.body: The order you install trim affects joint visibility and efficiency. Plan the sequence before you start.
steps: Identify primary sight lines (main entry, focal points)
Plan to work away from primary sight lines — joints face away from view
For baseboard: start at door casings, work around room
For crown: start at focal wall, work outward
Plan scarf joint locations over studs, away from sight lines
Determine cope vs miter strategy for inside corners

procedures.title: Material Calculation
procedures.body: Accurate takeoffs prevent mid-job supply runs and ensure consistent material from the same lot.
steps: Measure total linear feet of each trim type needed
Add waste factor: 10% for straight runs, 15% for complex rooms
Round up to nearest stock length
Add extra for setup cuts and test pieces
Order 5-10% extra from same lot for repairs/callbacks

callouts.content: Stock lengths are typically 8', 10', 12', or 16'. Plan cuts to minimize waste — a 14' wall is better served by one 16' piece than two 8' pieces with a scarf joint.

procedures.title: Dealing with Out-of-Square Conditions
procedures.body: Real-world rooms are rarely perfectly square. Plan your approach before cutting.
steps: Identify the worst corners (most out of square)
Decide: split the difference or favor one side
For miters: adjust angles by half the error
For copes: the coped piece follows the wall regardless of angle
Back-cut miters slightly to ensure tight face fit
Document angles for future reference

callouts.content: When corners are badly out of square (>2° off 90°), coped inside corners are more forgiving than miters. The cope follows the wall plane naturally.

title: Cutting & Joinery
introduction: Clean, accurate cuts are the foundation of professional trim work. This module covers the cutting techniques for every joint type you'll encounter.
learningObjectives: Set up and calibrate miter saw for accurate cuts
Execute clean miter joints at various angles
Perform coped joints for inside corners
Cut scarf joints for splicing long runs
Troubleshoot common cutting problems

criticalStandards.description: Always verify miter saw is square (90° crosscut, 45° miter) before starting — check with known square
Support long stock to prevent binding and kickback — never let unsupported ends droop
Cope cuts must be back-cut at least 5° to ensure tight face fit

procedures.title: Miter Saw Setup & Calibration
procedures.body: A miter saw that's even slightly out of calibration produces joints that won't close. Verify setup before every job.
steps: Clean sawdust from miter detents and pivot points
Set saw to 0° (90° crosscut)
Cut test piece from scrap
Check cut against known square — adjust if needed
Set saw to 45° left, cut test piece
Set saw to 45° right, cut test piece
Place 45° cuts together — should form perfect 90°
Verify blade is sharp — dull blades tear and burn
Check fence is straight and secure

callouts.content: A blade that's 0.5° out of square produces a gap that opens 1/16" over a 6" piece. Always verify calibration before starting.

procedures.title: Miter Cuts (Outside Corners)
procedures.body: Outside corners are mitered. The angle is typically 45° for 90° corners, but real corners are rarely perfect.
steps: Measure corner angle with angle finder
Divide angle by 2 for miter setting (90° corner = 45° each piece)
Mark piece at exact length on the long point
Position material flat on saw table (baseboard) or against fence (crown)
Align blade to waste side of mark
Hold material firmly — never cross hands over blade path
Let blade reach full speed before cutting
Cut in single smooth motion
Check fit before moving to next piece

tables.caption: Miter Angles for Common Corner Angles

procedures.title: Coped Joints (Inside Corners)
procedures.body: Coped joints handle inside corners better than miters because they follow the wall plane and hide gaps from seasonal movement.
steps: Cut first piece square, run it into the corner
Cut second piece at 45° to expose the profile
Use coping saw to cut along the profile line
Back-cut at 5-10° angle — remove material behind the face
Test fit against scrap piece of same profile
Adjust with file or sandpaper until tight fit
Final piece should slip over first piece with no gaps at face

callouts.content: The cope cut must be back-cut. If the back of the cope touches before the face, you get a gap at the visible surface. Always remove more material behind the face.
Highlight the profile line with pencil before coping — it's easier to follow a marked line than the raw miter edge.

procedures.title: Scarf Joints (Splicing Long Runs)
procedures.body: When stock isn't long enough for a single piece, scarf joints create an invisible splice.
steps: Locate splice over a stud (must nail both sides)
Cut first piece at 30-45° angle, long point toward room
Cut second piece at matching angle
Apply wood glue to both mating surfaces
Install first piece, nail before and after joint
Install second piece, aligning faces perfectly
Nail through joint into stud
Sand joint lightly if needed

callouts.content: Scarf joints should face away from the primary entry or sight line. When you walk into a room, you should never see the 'open' side of a scarf joint.

fields.label: CS-TRM-007: Always verify miter saw is square (90° crosscut, 45° miter) ...

title: Baseboard Installation
introduction: Baseboard is the most common trim installation. Proper technique here sets the standard for all your trim work.
learningObjectives: Install baseboard with consistent reveals and tight joints
Handle out-of-level floors and out-of-plumb walls
Execute proper inside and outside corner treatments
Install shoe molding and quarter round

criticalStandards.description: Baseboard must be tight to floor OR consistent gap with shoe molding — never random gaps
Nail baseboard to studs (not just drywall) with 15-16 gauge finish nails — minimum two nails per stud

procedures.title: Baseboard Layout
procedures.body: Plan the installation sequence to minimize visible joints and optimize workflow.
steps: Identify starting point (usually at door casing)
Mark stud locations on wall — light pencil at top where baseboard will cover
Identify inside corners (cope or miter)
Identify outside corners (miter)
Plan scarf joint locations over studs if needed
Determine if shoe molding is needed to cover floor gaps

procedures.title: Installing to Door Casings
procedures.body: Where baseboard meets door casing, the joint must be tight and properly aligned.
steps: Measure from door casing to corner
Cut baseboard square at casing end
Cut opposite end for corner (cope or miter)
Test fit — should be snug against casing with no gap
If baseboard is thicker than casing: scribe and plane edge
If baseboard is thinner than casing: acceptable as-is
Nail baseboard to studs, starting at casing end

callouts.content: The baseboard-to-casing joint is highly visible. Take extra time to ensure this joint is perfect — it's the first thing people notice.

procedures.title: Inside Corner Treatment
procedures.body: Inside corners can be coped or mitered. Coped joints are more reliable for paint-grade work.
steps: First piece: cut square, run into corner
Nail first piece in place
Second piece: cope to fit over first piece profile
Test fit cope against installed piece
Adjust cope as needed until tight fit
Nail second piece, starting at coped end

callouts.title: Coping Strategy
callouts.content: For rooms with multiple inside corners, work around the room so you're always coping the piece you're installing onto the piece already in place. Never try to cope both ends of a single piece.

procedures.title: Outside Corner Treatment
procedures.body: Outside corners are always mitered. The key is tight face contact even if the corner isn't perfectly square.
steps: Measure corner angle with angle finder
Cut first piece at half the corner angle (typically 45°)
Install first piece, nail except near corner
Cut second piece at matching angle
Dry fit — adjust angle if needed
Apply wood glue to miter faces
Install second piece, align faces perfectly
Nail through corner to lock joint
Wipe excess glue immediately

callouts.content: Back-cut miters slightly (1-2°) to ensure the face closes tight. A miter that touches at the back before the face will show a gap.

procedures.title: Shoe Molding Installation
procedures.body: Shoe molding (or quarter round) covers the gap between baseboard and floor. It's especially useful over hard flooring that requires expansion gaps.
steps: Install all baseboard first
Measure and cut shoe molding
Inside corners: cope or miter
Outside corners: miter
Nail shoe to baseboard only — never to floor
Use 18-gauge brad nails, angled slightly upward
Set nails and fill

callouts.content: Never nail shoe molding to the floor. The shoe must float with the baseboard while the floor moves independently. Nailing to the floor can crack the shoe when the floor expands.

title: Door & Window Casing
introduction: Door and window casing frames the most prominent features in a room. Consistent reveals and tight joints are essential.
learningObjectives: Install door casing with consistent reveals
Handle out-of-square jambs and openings
Install window casing including stool and apron
Execute picture frame and Craftsman-style casing

criticalStandards.description: Maintain consistent reveal (typically 3/16" to 1/4") from jamb edge to casing edge on all sides
Head casing miter joints must align perfectly with leg casing — no step or gap visible

procedures.title: Setting Reveal Lines
procedures.body: The reveal is the small step between the jamb face and the casing. Consistent reveals are a hallmark of quality trim work.
steps: Determine reveal dimension (typically 3/16" to 1/4")
Set combination square to reveal distance
Mark reveal line on head jamb
Mark reveal line on both side jambs
Verify reveal marks form square corners
Adjust if jamb is out of square

callouts.content: A reveal gauge speeds up marking. Set it once and use it consistently across all openings for uniform appearance.

procedures.title: Door Casing Installation (Mitered)
procedures.body: Standard mitered door casing with head piece and two leg pieces.
steps: Measure head jamb reveal-to-reveal
Cut head casing with 45° miters at both ends, long point to long point
Tack head casing in place with one nail
Measure leg length from floor to head casing miter
Cut leg casing with 45° miter at top, square at bottom
Test fit leg to head — adjust miter if needed
Apply glue to miter joint
Nail leg in place, starting at miter
Repeat for opposite leg
Secure head casing fully
Pin miters with 23-gauge pin or brad

callouts.content: Work from the head casing down. This allows you to fine-tune leg length to achieve perfect miter alignment at the head.

procedures.title: Window Casing with Stool & Apron
procedures.body: Traditional window casing includes a stool (interior sill) and apron below the stool.
steps: Measure rough opening and window frame
Cut stool to length — extends past casing width plus horn (typically 1")
Notch stool to fit around window frame
Level and install stool — shim if needed
Mark reveal lines on jambs
Install head casing
Install leg casings down to stool
Measure apron length (matches head casing length)
Cut apron with returned ends or square ends
Install apron tight under stool, centered

procedures.title: Craftsman/Butt Joint Casing
procedures.body: Craftsman style uses butt joints with the head casing sitting on top of the legs, often with a slight overhang.
steps: Measure and install leg casings first (square cuts top and bottom)
Determine head casing overhang (typically 1/4" to 1/2")
Cut head casing to span legs plus overhang on each end
Install head casing, resting on legs
Nail through head into leg tops for solid joint
Fill nail holes

callouts.content: Craftsman casing is more forgiving of out-of-square openings because butt joints can accommodate angle variations better than miters.

title: Crown Molding
introduction: Crown molding is the most challenging trim to install because it sits at an angle between wall and ceiling. Understanding the geometry is essential before making your first cut.
learningObjectives: Understand crown molding geometry and spring angles
Install crown using jig-based or flat-cutting methods
Execute inside and outside corner joints
Handle out-of-level ceilings and out-of-plumb walls

criticalStandards.description: Crown spring angle must match crown profile — 38° or 45° typical. Wrong angle creates gaps
Test all crown cuts with scrap pieces before cutting actual material

procedures.title: Understanding Crown Geometry
procedures.body: Crown molding has a spring angle — the angle it projects from the wall/ceiling corner. Most crown is either 38° or 45° spring angle.

callouts.title: Check Spring Angle
callouts.content: Before cutting, verify your crown's spring angle. Place it in the wall/ceiling corner — if it doesn't sit flat on both surfaces, you have the wrong angle setting.

procedures.title: Cutting Crown — Nested Method
procedures.body: In the nested method, the crown sits on the saw upside-down and backwards, as if the saw table is the ceiling and the fence is the wall.
steps: Build or buy a crown stop for consistent positioning
Position crown upside-down: ceiling edge on table, wall edge against fence
Adjust crown until it sits at correct spring angle
Secure crown stop to lock position
For inside corner (left): swing blade right, ceiling on left
For inside corner (right): swing blade left, ceiling on right
For outside corner (left): swing blade left, ceiling on left
For outside corner (right): swing blade right, ceiling on right

callouts.title: Remember: Upside Down and Backwards
callouts.content: The most common crown cutting mistake is forgetting the orientation. The ceiling side is ALWAYS on the saw table. The bottom of the crown (facing the room) is facing UP at you.

procedures.title: Cutting Crown — Compound Miter Method
procedures.body: With compound miter settings, crown can be cut flat on the table. This requires knowing the miter and bevel angles for your spring angle.

callouts.content: The compound method is useful when crown is too large for the saw's nested capacity. However, the nested method is generally easier to visualize and execute.

procedures.title: Inside Corner — Coped
procedures.body: Coped inside corners are more reliable than mitered, especially if walls aren't perfectly square.
steps: Cut first piece square, install into corner
Cut second piece at inside miter to reveal profile
Cope along the profile line with coping saw
Back-cut 5-10° to ensure face contact
Test fit against scrap piece
Install, sliding coped end over first piece

procedures.title: Outside Corner — Mitered
procedures.body: Outside corners are always mitered. The compound geometry makes these joints challenging.
steps: Measure corner angle (usually 90°)
Cut first piece at outside miter
Install first piece, nail except near corner
Cut second piece at opposite outside miter
Dry fit — check for tight face joint
Apply glue to miter faces
Install second piece, align carefully
Nail to lock joint

callouts.content: Always test crown cuts with scrap pieces before cutting actual material. Crown corners are easy to visualize incorrectly.

title: Nailing & Fastening
introduction: Proper fastening keeps trim in place, closes joints, and prevents callbacks. Understanding nailer setup and nail selection ensures professional results.
learningObjectives: Select appropriate fastener type and gauge for each application
Set up and adjust pneumatic nailers for consistent results
Identify proper nailing patterns and stud-hitting techniques
Troubleshoot common nailing problems

criticalStandards.description: 15-16 gauge finish nails for structural attachment to studs; 18 gauge for detail attachment
Nail length = material thickness + 1" minimum penetration into substrate
Never nail closer than 3/4" to the end of a board — pre-drill or use adhesive

procedures.title: Fastener Selection
procedures.body: Different applications require different fastener types and sizes.

procedures.title: Nailer Setup & Adjustment
procedures.body: Proper nailer setup ensures consistent depth without blowouts or proud nails.
steps: Connect to appropriate air pressure (70-120 PSI typical)
Adjust depth on test piece of same material
Nail should set just below surface (1/32")
If nail is proud: increase pressure or depth setting
If nail blows through: decrease pressure or depth setting
Test on scrap from each material type — settings differ by density
Re-verify after changing nail length

callouts.content: MDF requires lower pressure than solid wood. High pressure blows through MDF and creates craters that can't be filled cleanly.

procedures.title: Nailing Patterns
procedures.body: Consistent nailing patterns ensure trim stays tight to the wall and joints stay closed.
steps: Mark stud locations on wall before installing trim
Baseboard: two nails per stud — one 3/4" from top, one 3/4" from bottom
Casing: one nail every 12" along jamb edge, one along wall edge
Crown: nail both wall edge (into studs) and ceiling edge (into top plate)
At outside miters: nail through the joint to lock pieces together
Near ends: stay 3/4" back or pre-drill to prevent splits

procedures.title: Troubleshooting Nailing Problems
procedures.body: Common issues and their solutions.

tables.caption: Nailing Troubleshooting
rows: Pressure too low, depth too shallow
Decrease pressure, switch to smaller gauge
Nail too long, hitting hard material
Use shorter nail, verify stud location
Pre-drill, use glue instead, stay 3/4" back
Verify stud locations, use longer nails

title: Filling & Caulking
introduction: The filling and caulking stage transforms carpentry into finish work. This is where gaps disappear and joints become seamless.
learningObjectives: Select appropriate filler for each application
Apply wood filler and spackle for invisible nail holes
Execute professional caulk joints
Understand the relationship between trim, caulk, and paint

criticalStandards.description: Stainable filler for stain-grade trim; sandable filler for paint-grade — never mix
Caulk is not a gap filler — gaps over 1/4" require backer rod or multiple passes

procedures.title: Filler Selection
procedures.body: Different fillers serve different purposes. Match the filler to the application and finish type.

tables.caption: Filler Types and Applications
rows: Nail holes, small gaps in paint-grade

procedures.title: Filling Nail Holes
procedures.body: Invisible nail hole repairs require the right technique, not just the right product.
steps: Verify nails are set 1/32" below surface
If proud, use nail set to sink
Apply filler with putty knife — slightly overfill
Wipe excess immediately
Allow to dry per product instructions
Sand flush with 150-180 grit
Wipe dust before painting

callouts.content: For paint-grade work, lightweight spackle is fastest. It dries in minutes, sands easily, and is ready for primer. Don't overthink nail holes on paint-grade trim.

procedures.title: Caulking Technique
procedures.body: Good caulk joints are smooth, consistent, and appropriate for the application.
steps: Cut caulk tube at 45° angle, small opening (1/8")
Apply steady bead along joint
Tool immediately with wet finger or caulk tool
Work in 3-4 foot sections — caulk skins quickly
Maintain consistent bead width
Wipe excess on damp rag
Allow to dry 24 hours before painting

callouts.content: Only caulk joints that will be painted. Caulk yellows over time and cannot be stained. Never caulk stain-grade trim.

procedures.title: Where to Caulk vs Where to Fill
procedures.body: Not every gap requires the same treatment.

tables.caption: Caulk vs Filler Decision Guide
rows: Flexible joint, surfaces may move independently
Rigid joint, same material both sides

fields.label: 4. Where to Caulk vs Where to Fill

title: Paint Preparation
introduction: The line between trim carpentry and painting isn't always clear. Understanding paint preparation ensures your trim work shows well under finish coats.
learningObjectives: Prepare trim for painter or finish work
Understand primer requirements for different materials
Execute final sanding and dust removal
Coordinate trim completion with painting schedule

criticalStandards.description: All bare wood and MDF must be primed before topcoat — including cut ends
Final sand with 150-180 grit — coarser leaves visible scratches through paint

procedures.title: Pre-Prime Requirements
procedures.body: Some materials and situations require priming before installation.

procedures.title: Final Sanding
procedures.body: Final sanding creates a smooth surface for paint adhesion.
steps: Verify all filler is fully dry
Sand filled areas flush with surrounding surface
Sand entire trim with 150-180 grit
Pay attention to profiles — don't round edges
Sand with the grain on visible faces
Remove all sanding dust with tack cloth or vacuum
Inspect under raking light for missed spots

callouts.content: Don't over-sand factory-primed trim. The factory primer is already smooth — heavy sanding removes it and creates more work.

procedures.title: Dust Removal
procedures.body: Dust left on trim creates bumps under paint.
steps: Vacuum all surfaces and joints with brush attachment
Wipe with tack cloth or damp microfiber
Wipe top edges of baseboard (dust settles here)
Clean out inside corners
Wipe window stools and any horizontal surfaces
Allow to dry if using damp cloth before painting

procedures.title: Coordination with Painters
procedures.body: Trim and paint schedules must align for best results.

callouts.content: The best results come when trim is installed after walls are primed. This allows caulk to seal against a stable surface and prevents trim paint from getting on raw drywall.

title: Quality Inspection & Defect Prevention
introduction: A systematic quality inspection catches issues before the painter arrives or the customer does. Taking time now prevents callbacks later.
learningObjectives: Perform systematic quality inspection of completed trim work
Identify common defects and their causes
Understand acceptable tolerances and visual standards
Document and address punch list items

criticalStandards.description: No visible gaps at joints when viewed from 6 feet at normal lighting
Reveals must be consistent within 1/32" around each opening

procedures.title: Visual Inspection Protocol
procedures.body: Inspect all trim work systematically before calling the job complete.

checkpoints.acceptable: Joints closed, no visible gaps at 6'
Slightly below surface, ready for fill

checkpoints.unacceptable: Trim deviates from wall, gaps behind

procedures.title: Common Defects & Prevention
procedures.body: Understanding defect causes helps prevent them in future work.

tables.caption: Trim Defect Matrix
rows: Out of square cut, wood movement, no glue
Verify saw square, glue joints, acclimate
Scribe to wall, use flexible trim, caulk
Caulk if minor, re-install if severe
Insufficient fill, wrong filler, no sand
Poor marking, jamb out of square
Nails missed framing, no glue at joints
Verify framing location, glue all joints
Fill if minor, replace if structural

procedures.title: Punch List Documentation
procedures.body: Document all items requiring attention before final sign-off.
steps: Walk entire job with consistent lighting
Note defects by room and location
Photograph significant issues
Categorize: must fix vs. acceptable vs. customer preference
Address must-fix items before final walkthrough
Review with customer if preference items exist

procedures.title: Tolerance Standards
procedures.body: Know what's acceptable and what requires correction.
